content:
# Genesis Chapter 29 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16r–16v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_029_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_029_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029 (page 25), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0030 (page 26)

---

## The Well, the Wife He Wanted, and the Wife He Got

**1** Jacob picked up his feet and traveled east, into the land of the eastern peoples.

**2** Looking out, he saw a well in the open country. Three flocks of sheep were resting beside it, for this was the well the shepherds used to water their flocks. A great stone covered the mouth of the well. **3** Only when all the flocks had gathered there would the shepherds roll the stone from the mouth of the well and water the animals. Then they would roll the stone back into place over the mouth.

**4** Jacob said to the shepherds there, "My friends — where are you from?"

"From Haran," they said.

**5** "Do you know a man named Laban, the son of Nahor?"

"We know him," they said.

**6** "Is he well?"

"He is well. And look — here comes his daughter Rachel with the flock right now."

**7** Jacob said, "But the sun is still high! It's too early to bring the animals in for the night. Water the flocks and take them back out to graze."

**8** "We can't," they answered. "Not until every flock is here and the stone is rolled from the well. Only then can we water the sheep."

---

**9** While Jacob was still speaking with them, Rachel arrived with her father's flock, for she was a shepherdess.

**10** The moment Jacob saw Rachel — daughter of his mother's brother Laban, with the flock of his uncle Laban — he stepped forward, rolled the stone away from the mouth of the well single-handed, and watered the flock of his uncle Laban.

**11** Then Jacob kissed Rachel and wept aloud.

**12** He told her he was her father's kinsman, that he was Rebekah's son. She ran and told her father.

---

**13** As soon as Laban heard the news about his sister's son Jacob, he ran out to meet him. He embraced him and kissed him and brought him into his house. Jacob told him everything that had happened, **14** and Laban said, "You are truly bone of my bone and flesh of my flesh." And Jacob stayed with him a full month.

---

**15** After that, Laban said to Jacob, "You're my own relative — but that doesn't mean you should be working for me for nothing. Tell me what you want your wages to be."

**16** Now Laban had two daughters. The older was named Leah and the younger was named Rachel. **17** Leah's eyes were soft, but Rachel had a beautiful figure and a beautiful face.

**18** Jacob had fallen in love with Rachel. So he said, "I'll work for you seven years in exchange for your younger daughter Rachel."

**19** Laban said, "Better that I give her to you than to some outsider. Stay with me."

**20** So Jacob worked seven years for Rachel — and they felt to him like just a few days, because of how much he loved her.

---

**21** Then Jacob said to Laban, "The time has come. Give me my wife — I've served my time. I want to be with her."

**22** So Laban gathered all the men of the place and threw a feast. **23** But when night fell, he took his daughter Leah and brought her in to Jacob, and Jacob slept with her. **24** (Laban had given his slave woman Zilpah to his daughter Leah as her servant.)

**25** When morning came — it was Leah.

"What have you done to me?" Jacob demanded of Laban. "Didn't I work for you for Rachel? Why have you deceived me?"

**26** Laban said, "It isn't done here — giving the younger daughter in marriage before the firstborn. **27** Complete this wedding week, and we'll give you the other one as well, in exchange for another seven years of work."

**28** Jacob agreed. He completed the week with Leah, and then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel as his wife. **29** (Laban had given his slave woman Bilhah to his daughter Rachel as her servant.)

**30** Jacob slept with Rachel too. He loved Rachel more than he loved Leah, and he worked for Laban another seven years.

---

**31** When the LORD saw that Leah was unloved, he opened her womb. But Rachel could not conceive.

**32** Leah became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She named him Reuben, saying, "The LORD has seen my suffering. Surely now my husband will love me."

**33** She became pregnant again and gave birth to a son, and said, "Because the LORD heard that I am unloved, he has given me this one too." She named him Simeon.

**34** She became pregnant again and gave birth to a son, and said, "Now at last my husband will be joined to me — I have given him three sons." So she named him Levi.

**35** She became pregnant one more time and gave birth to a son, and said, "This time I will praise the LORD." So she named him Judah.

And she stopped bearing children.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The shape of the chapter

Genesis 29 moves in three acts: the arrival at the well (vv.1-14), the marriage negotiations and deception (vv.15-30), and the beginnings of the tribal family through Leah's children (vv.31-35). It is a chapter built on echoes — the well scene mirrors Genesis 24, the name etymologies mirror Genesis 4, and the deception mirrors Genesis 27. Jacob, who deceived his blind father with a false identity, is himself deceived by his father-in-law in the dark. The deceiver gets deceived. The text does not moralize about this; it simply narrates it, and the reader who knows Jacob's history feels the poetic weight of it.

---

### Verse 1 — "lifted his feet"

**וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו** — literally, "Jacob lifted his feet." This is an unusual Hebrew idiom found only here in the Bible. Most translators smooth it to "Jacob continued his journey" or "set out." But the image is vivid and worth preserving in some form. I have rendered it as "picked up his feet" — slightly idiomatic but retaining the physical, purposeful energy of the original. The Vaticanus LXX renders the phrase as **ἄρας δὲ Ιακωβ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ** — "Jacob, lifting his feet" — following the Hebrew exactly. After the extraordinary stillness of the Bethel dream (Jacob lying on the ground, the world opened up above him), this verse marks the resumption of movement. He has been spoken to by God. Now he walks.

---

### Verse 10 — Jacob at the well: the type-scene

The betrothal type-scene appears three times in the Pentateuch: a man travels to a foreign land, meets a woman at a well, waters animals, goes to the woman's household, and an engagement follows (Genesis 24: Abraham's servant and Rebekah; Genesis 29: Jacob and Rachel; Exodus 2: Moses and Zipporah). The pattern is recognizable enough that ancient readers would have understood its shape the moment Jacob reached the well. What makes the Genesis 29 version remarkable is its explosion of energy: Jacob doesn't wait for instructions or assistance. The moment he sees Rachel, he single-handedly rolls away a stone that normally takes all the gathered shepherds to move. The Hebrew syntax piles up verbs — *saw, rolled, watered* — with no pause for breath. He is performing a feat of strength as an act of attraction. The whole courtship compressed into a single muscular gesture.

---

### Verse 11 — kissing and weeping

**וַיִּשַּׁק יַעֲקֹב לְרָחֵל וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-קֹלוֹ וַיֵּבְךְּ** — "Jacob kissed Rachel and lifted his voice and wept." The kiss in the ancient Near East between kin is a greeting, not necessarily a romantic gesture, though the erotic undertones in this particular meeting are unmistakable from the context. What is most striking is the weeping. A strong young man who has just performed a feat of strength at the well immediately breaks into loud tears when he kisses the woman he has just impressed. The reason is implied by v.12 and v.13: Jacob is overwhelmed by arrival. He is a fugitive, far from home, and here in a foreign land he has found family — the daughter of the very house his mother came from. It is not just attraction; it is relief, recognition, and perhaps grief for everything he has left behind. He is safe. He has arrived.

The Vaticanus LXX preserves both actions: **κατεφίλησεν Ιακωβ τὴν Ραχηλ καὶ βοήσας τῇ φωνῇ αὐτοῦ ἔκλαυσεν** — he kissed Rachel and, crying out with his voice, wept.

---

### Verse 14 — "bone of my bone"

**אַךְ עַצְמִי וּבְשָׂרִי אָתָּה** — "Surely you are my bone and my flesh." This is the covenant formula of kinship — the same language Adam uses of Eve in Genesis 2:23, the same formula used in the covenant of the tribes in 2 Samuel 5:1 and 19:13. Laban is declaring Jacob family, recognizing him as a legitimate member of the household, bound by the obligations of blood. It is an affirmation that carries weight. Unfortunately, as the chapter soon makes clear, Laban's sense of family obligation runs in one direction only: he will extract labor from his kinsman with considerable ingenuity.

---

### Verse 17 — Leah's eyes

**וְעֵינֵי לֵאָה רַכּוֹת** — "And Leah's eyes were *rakkot*." The word **רַכּוֹת** (*rakkot*) means soft, tender, or delicate. In the context of a physical description preceding a contrast with Rachel's striking beauty, most ancient interpreters (and many modern ones) read this as a defect: Leah's eyes were weak, dull, or failing — perhaps she was poor-sighted or pale-eyed in an age when dark, bright eyes were the standard of attractiveness. Other interpreters read it as a positive: her eyes were gentle, tender, kind — perhaps the most beautiful feature about her, standing against Rachel's obvious physical perfection.

The Vaticanus LXX uses **ἀσθενεῖς** (*astheneis*) — "weak, without strength" — which supports the defect reading. But the ambiguity in the Hebrew is real, and I have chosen the deliberately neutral "soft," which allows both readings to stand. Leah, in any case, is defined here by her contrast with Rachel: the unloved sister next to the beautiful one. It is a quietly devastating verse. The chapter will show that Leah is the one through whom the covenant line runs — through her sons Levi and Judah — while Rachel, loved passionately, struggles to conceive. The text's sympathies are not simple.

---

### Verse 20 — the great love statement

**וַיִּהְיוּ בְעֵינָיו כְּיָמִים אֲחָדִים בְּאַהֲבָתוֹ אֹתָהּ** — "And they were in his eyes like a few days because of his love for her."

This is one of the most beautiful sentences in the Bible. Seven years of labor — the prime years of a man's physical life, given over entirely to another man's household — and the text says simply: they felt like a few days. Because he loved her. No other explanation is given or needed. The economy of the sentence performs what it describes: years compress to days in the telling just as they compressed in Jacob's experience. You blink and seven years are gone because love makes time irrelevant.

I have kept the verse spare for exactly this reason. The tendency in dynamic translation is to expand and explain. Here, restraint is the only faithful option.

---

### Verse 25 — "it was Leah"

**וְהִנֵּה-הִוא לֵאָה** — "And look — she was Leah."

The most structurally perfect verse in the chapter. Three Hebrew words. The exclamation particle **הִנֵּה** (*hinneh*) marks the sudden shock of recognition — "behold!" — the same word used when Jacob woke at Bethel to find the LORD present where he had not expected him. Now he wakes again to a surprise: not the divine presence but a betrayal. The man who deceived his blind father by disguising himself as his brother discovers, in the morning light, that he has been deceived in the dark. He married the wrong sister. The chapter's irony is not gentle: the one who pulled the wool over his father's eyes has had the wool pulled over his.

The Vaticanus LXX reads: **ἦν δὲ Λεια** — "but it was Leah" — a flat declarative that captures the same cold-water shock of recognition.

---

### Verses 26-27 — Laban's justification

Laban's explanation is smooth and reveals nothing of guilt: "It isn't the custom here to give the younger before the firstborn." The word translated "firstborn" (**בְּכִירָה**, *bekhirah*) is the feminine of **בְּכוֹר** (*bekhor*) — the firstborn son, the word associated with birthright and blessing throughout the Jacob story. Jacob traded in birthright; now birthright exacts its price from him. He wanted to bypass the elder for the younger, as he himself had been the younger who bypassed the elder. Laban, with supreme irony, invokes the very principle Jacob violated.

The additional seven years demanded by Laban are brutal in their logic: Jacob must pay twice what he bargained for. That he agrees — that he completes the week and works another seven years — is the measure of how deeply he loved Rachel. He has no leverage; Laban holds all the cards. Jacob, the clever one, the one who always found an angle, accepts the terms with no recorded complaint.

---

### Verse 31 — "the LORD saw that Leah was unloved"

**וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי-שְׂנוּאָה לֵאָה** — "And the LORD saw that Leah was *senu'ah*."

The word **שְׂנוּאָה** is the passive participle of **שָׂנֵא** — "to hate." Literally: "Leah was hated." Most translations soften this to "unloved" or "not loved," and in the context of a comparison with a passionately loved rival wife, "unloved" probably captures the social reality accurately. Jacob did not actively despise Leah; he simply did not love her the way he loved Rachel, and in the comparative world of polygamy, that difference registered as rejection. The Vaticanus LXX uses **ἐμισεῖτο** — "was being hated" — following the Hebrew's strength.

What is theologically significant is the subject of this sentence: *the LORD saw*. The LORD's attention is drawn not to the beloved, the chosen, the celebrated — but to the overlooked one. To the woman who is there because she was substituted for her sister in the dark. This divine noticing — **וַיַּרְא יְהוָה** — will be the engine of the rest of the chapter. God opens Leah's womb. He sees what Jacob cannot or will not see.

---

### Verses 32-35 — the naming of the four sons

The etymology-names of Leah's first four sons are a compressed emotional autobiography:

**Reuben** — from **רְאֵה** (*re'eh*, "see") and **בֵּן** (*ben*, "son"), or more directly from **רָאָה בְּעָנְיִי** (*ra'ah be'onyi*, "he has seen my affliction"). The first word out of Leah's mouth when she names her firstborn is *affliction*. She is not celebrating; she is pleading. "Surely now," she says, "my husband will love me." He does not.

**Simeon** — from **שָׁמַע** (*shama'*, "heard"). The LORD heard that she was unloved. She is still unloved, and the naming is still addressed to the LORD, not to Jacob. Jacob has not turned toward her. Only God has.

**Levi** — from **לָוָה** (*lavah*, "to be joined, attached"). "Now *at last* my husband will be joined to me — I have given him three sons." The desperation is visible in the *now at last*. She has given Jacob three sons and he is still not joined to her the way she wants. She is narrating her own longing through her children's names.

**Judah** — from **יָדָה** (*yadah*, "to give thanks, to praise"). Something has shifted. She does not say "now he will love me" or "now he will be joined to me." She says only: "I will praise the LORD." She has stopped making her happiness contingent on Jacob's affection. She names this child for praise alone. The fourth name is the breakthrough — Leah, still unloved by her husband, has found a place where she can stand: gratitude to God.

It is through Judah — the child of the unwanted wife, the child named for pure praise — that the messianic line will run.

The Leningrad Codex page 2 (folio 16v) carries the naming sequence in the right column, with the characteristic Masoretic cantillation marks (tifha, etnahta) clearly visible. The parasha division marker after verse 35 is visible in the margin, indicating the close of this literary unit before Genesis 30 begins.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16r–16v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 29 occupies the right columns of folio 16r (verses 1–22, following the end of Genesis 28) and the opening columns of folio 16v (verses 23–35, continuing into Genesis 30). Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 25–26 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029 and /0030). The Vaticanus LXX follows the Hebrew text faithfully throughout Genesis 29 with no significant omissions. Notable observations from the Greek: v.1 — Vaticanus follows the unusual idiom literally (**ἄρας τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ**, "lifting his feet"); v.17 — **ἀσθενεῖς** ("weak") for the eyes of Leah, supporting the defect reading over the "gentle/soft" reading; v.25 — **ἦν δὲ Λεια** ("but it was Leah"), the flat recognition statement; v.31 — **ἐμισεῖτο** ("was being hated"), following the Hebrew's stronger word rather than softening. No DSS fragments of Genesis 29 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
